asset_categories.name: Frontend / Client User Interface
asset_categories.description: Visual components that render HTML/CSS/JS (React, Vue, Angular, Svelte, server-side templates). They handle user interactions in the browser: forms, DOM events, client-side routing, and dynamic content rendering.

asset_categories.name: Client-side Storage
asset_categories.description: Browser-based storage mechanisms: localStorage, sessionStorage, IndexedDB, Cache API, and cookies. They persist or cache client-side data between requests or sessions.

asset_categories.name: Frontend HTTP Client / API Client Layer
asset_categories.description: Browser-side HTTP client layer: axios instances, fetch wrappers, and API service modules running in the browser. They construct requests, inject authorization headers (Bearer tokens, cookies), handle authentication errors (401/403), centralize base URL and interceptor configuration, and process API responses. Unlike backend controllers, they run in an untrusted environment with all logic visible to the user and must never store secrets or perform authorization decisions.

asset_categories.name: Frontend Router / Client-side Navigation Guard
asset_categories.description: Client-side routing and navigation guard components: React Router, Vue Router, Angular Router, PrivateRoute/AuthGuard wrappers, and access control checks based on in-memory or localStorage state. They enforce UI-level routing decisions. These controls are presentation-layer only and can be bypassed by the user; they must never be the sole authorization mechanism — all authorization must also be enforced server-side.

asset_categories.name: Frontend Realtime Client / WebSocket & SSE Client
asset_categories.description: WebSocket, Socket.io-client, and Server-Sent Events (SSE) consumers running in the browser. They establish persistent connections to backend services, transmit authentication tokens in the initial handshake (query param or header), and process streaming events in real time. Security concerns include token exposure in connection URLs, event injection from untrusted or spoofed sources, missing WSS enforcement, and absence of origin validation.

asset_categories.name: API Gateway / Reverse Proxy / Edge Layer
asset_categories.description: HTTP/S traffic entry layer: API Gateways (Kong, AWS API Gateway, NGINX), reverse proxies, load balancers, WAFs, and BFF (Backend-for-Frontend) components. They handle routing, throttling, TLS termination, security headers, and enforcement of perimeter authentication policies.

asset_categories.name: Backend Application Controllers / Request Handlers
asset_categories.description: Backend application controllers, handlers, and endpoints that receive HTTP requests, execute coordination logic, and return responses. Includes REST controllers, GraphQL resolvers, gRPC handlers, and serverless functions (Lambda, Cloud Functions).

asset_categories.name: Backend Microservices / Internal Services
asset_categories.description: Decoupled internal services implementing specific business domains. They communicate with each other via HTTP, gRPC, events, or queues. Includes asynchronous workers, batch processing services, and internal daemons.

asset_categories.name: Authentication Service / Identity Provider
asset_categories.description: Components responsible for verifying the identity of users and devices: in-house login servers, internal Identity Providers (IdP), LDAP/AD integrations, MFA solutions, and credential management systems. May include external providers such as Auth0 or Keycloak when operated by the team.

asset_categories.name: Authorization Service / Policy Engine
asset_categories.description: Components that make access control decisions: policy engines (OPA, Casbin), RBAC/ABAC services, authorization middleware, and data access guards. They centralize and enforce rules on which consumer can access which resource.

asset_categories.name: Token Issuer / Authorization Server (OAuth AS / OIDC OP)
asset_categories.description: Server that issues, validates, and revokes security tokens: OAuth 2.0 Authorization Servers, OpenID Connect Providers, JWT/SAML issuers. Manages delegated authorization flows, refresh tokens, and PKCE.

asset_categories.name: Session Store / State Management Backend
asset_categories.description: Server-side storage for user session data: Redis, Memcached, or other databases used as session backends. Maintains session state referenced by opaque tokens (reference tokens).

asset_categories.name: Database / Persistence Layer
asset_categories.description: Persistent data storage systems: relational databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle), NoSQL (MongoDB, Cassandra, DynamoDB), search engines (Elasticsearch), and graph databases (Neo4j). Store the critical application state.

asset_categories.name: Cache / In-Memory Store
asset_categories.description: Application cache layers: Redis, Memcached, Varnish, CDN caches, and embedded caches. Store responses, query results, or data fragments to improve performance. May contain sensitive data if not correctly configured.

asset_categories.name: File Storage Service / Object Storage
asset_categories.description: Services for storing and managing files: local file systems, object storage (S3, GCS, Azure Blob), static content CDNs, and document management services. Receive, store, and serve files uploaded by users or generated by the application.

asset_categories.name: Message Broker / Event Bus
asset_categories.description: Asynchronous messaging infrastructure: Kafka, RabbitMQ, SQS/SNS, Azure Service Bus, NATS. Transports events and commands between decoupled services. Can propagate sensitive or malicious data if messages are not validated.

asset_categories.name: Secret Management Service / Key Vault
asset_categories.description: Systems for secure management of secrets and cryptographic material: HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager, and HSMs (Hardware Security Modules). Store and distribute credentials, API keys, certificates, and cryptographic keys.

asset_categories.name: WebRTC Infrastructure (TURN / SFU / MCU / Media Server)
asset_categories.description: Real-time communication infrastructure: TURN/STUN servers for NAT traversal, Selective Forwarding Units (SFUs), Multipoint Control Units (MCUs), recording servers, and media gateways. Handle real-time audio, video, and data streams.

asset_categories.name: External / Third-party Service Integration
asset_categories.description: Integrations with services external to the organization: payment APIs (Stripe, PayPal), email services (SendGrid, SES), maps, analytics, external OAuth providers (Google, GitHub), SMS services, and any third-party API consumed by the application.

asset_categories.name: Network Infrastructure / TLS Termination Layer
asset_categories.description: Network infrastructure managing encrypted communications: TLS terminators, firewalls, load balancers with certificate management, DNS, and internal network configurations (service mesh such as Istio/Linkerd). Controls which protocols and cipher suites are permitted.

asset_categories.name: CI/CD Pipeline / Build Infrastructure
asset_categories.description: Continuous integration and delivery pipelines: GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, CircleCI. Includes build, test, dependency analysis (SCA/SAST), artifact packaging, and deployment processes. A critical injection point for compromised dependencies and secrets.

asset_categories.name: Container & Orchestration Infrastructure
asset_categories.description: Container and orchestration infrastructure: Docker, Kubernetes (deployments, pods, namespaces), Helm charts, image registries (ECR, Docker Hub). Manages the application runtime environment, including configuration, environment variables, and resource limits.

asset_categories.name: Scheduled Job / Event-driven Worker / ETL Processor
asset_categories.description: Non-interactive backend processes triggered by time (cron, CloudWatch Events, Quartz) or cloud events (Lambda S3/SQS/SNS triggers, Azure Functions EventGrid). Includes ETL pipelines, data migration scripts run as jobs, and recurring batch processors. They run without an HTTP request context — there is no user session, no CSRF surface, and no interactive authentication. Credentials are injected via environment variables, IAM roles, or mounted secrets. Risk is concentrated in: secrets management, data injection via input files or queue messages, silent failure modes, and excessive privilege of the execution identity.

asset_categories.name: Admin Script / CLI Tool / Maintenance Utility
asset_categories.description: Operator-executed scripts, CLI tools, and maintenance utilities: database migration scripts, data repair scripts, one-off admin operations, internal CLI commands, and seed scripts. They run with elevated privileges (DBA accounts, admin API keys, superuser roles), are often excluded from code review, and rarely have audit logging. Risk is concentrated in: hardcoded credentials, privilege escalation, absence of audit trail for admin actions, and processing of sensitive data without data protection controls. Unlike scheduled_job, these are triggered manually by an operator rather than automatically.

asset_categories.name: Infrastructure as Code / Configuration as Code
asset_categories.description: Declarative infrastructure and configuration definitions: Terraform (.tf), Pulumi, AWS CloudFormation, Azure Bicep, Ansible playbooks, Helm values files, and Kubernetes manifests (Deployment, ServiceAccount, ClusterRole, NetworkPolicy, Secret). They define IAM roles, network boundaries, secret injection, and RBAC policies — making them the source of truth for privilege and access control in the environment. Security concerns are architectural rather than runtime: over-permissive IAM policies, hardcoded secrets in vars files, use of unverified modules from public registries, missing network policies, and absence of drift detection.
